title: Computation of Robust Dynamic Operating Envelopes Based on Non-convex OPF for Unbalanced Distribution Networks
summary: Introduces a sensitivity-filtered non-convex UTOPF workflow for robust DOE allocation, aiming to preserve network security under uncertainty while reducing computational burden.
key_equations: RDOE feasibility set: \mathcal{E}_t = \{u_t \mid \exists x_t: g(x_t,u_t,\xi_t) \le 0,\ \forall \xi_t \in \Xi_t\}
UTOPF objective template: \min_{x_t,u_t} f(x_t,u_t) \text{ s.t. } h(x_t,u_t)=0,\ g(x_t,u_t)\le0
assumptions: Three-phase unbalanced network model with AC non-convexity retained.
Uncertainty set over network parameters and DER utilization can be represented for robust feasibility checks.
contributions: Sensitivity-filtering strategy to prune low-impact dimensions in robust envelope computation.
Empirical comparison of fairness metrics in envelope allocation on Australian representative feeders.
claims: Non-convex/sensitivity-filtered method improves security fidelity vs linearized approximations.
Computation remains practical for operational envelope updates.
conclusions: Sensitivity filtering can preserve key security constraints while lowering solve time.
Fairness metric choice materially changes individual DER envelopes.
limitations: Validation is simulation-based on representative networks; broader deployment evidence is still needed.
Scalability to very large feeder portfolios is not fully characterized.
future_work: Integrate with real-time DSO workflows and state estimation updates.
Assess uncertainty calibration and robustness under forecast misspecification.
evidence: mandatory user link; full-text scraped via arXiv landing and related references

title: Linear OPF-Based Method for Computing Robust Dynamic Operating Envelopes for Distribution Networks
venue: Journal of Modern Power Systems and Clean Energy
summary: Presents a linearized robust DOE method to make envelope computation tractable for distribution operation and compares against non-robust baselines.
key_equations: Linearized sensitivity mapping: \Delta v \approx J_v \Delta p + K_v \Delta q
Robust linear constraints: A u \le b - \max_{\xi\in\Xi} B\xi
assumptions: Linearized power flow captures dominant network response.
Uncertainty set is polyhedral/box-like for tractable robust reformulation.
contributions: Linear OPF robust DOE pipeline with uncertainty handling.
Demonstrates speed/feasibility improvements for operations.
claims: Linear robust envelopes are practical for frequent recomputation.
Ignoring uncertainty degrades security margins.
conclusions: Linear robust DOE is computationally efficient but model-error sensitive.
Useful as a baseline against non-convex robust OPF.
limitations: Approximation error from linearization can misestimate feasible region boundaries.
future_work: Hybrid methods that retain nonlinear fidelity for high-sensitivity constraints.

title: Robust Dynamic Operating Envelopes for DER Integration in Unbalanced Distribution Networks
summary: Formulates robust envelopes for DER integration in unbalanced networks and quantifies security–utilization tradeoffs under uncertainty.
key_equations: Chance/robust template: \mathbb{P}(g(x,u,\xi)\le0)\ge1-\epsilon or \forall \xi\in\Xi, g(x,u,\xi)\le0
assumptions: Unbalanced feeder model and uncertainty over demand/generation realization.
Robustness level is policy-selected.
contributions: Formal robust DOE construction for practical DSO operation.
claims: Robust envelopes reduce risk of violations versus deterministic envelopes.
conclusions: Robustification is necessary when forecast and parameter errors are non-negligible.
limitations: Conservative settings can reduce DER utilization.
future_work: Adaptive uncertainty-set tuning with online feedback.
evidence: DOI metadata and cited in 2025 MDPI review reference section

title: Day-Ahead Dynamic Operating Envelopes in Low-Voltage Distribution Networks with Rooftop PVs: A Data-Driven Stochastic UTOPF Method
summary: Proposes stochastic UTOPF for day-ahead DOE scheduling using data-driven uncertainty representations for rooftop PV-heavy feeders.
key_equations: Stochastic OPF objective: \min \mathbb{E}_{\xi}[f(x,u,\xi)]
Scenario constraints: g(x_s,u,\xi_s)\le0,\  orall s\in\mathcal{S}
assumptions: Scenario set approximates uncertainty distribution.
Day-ahead forecasts available.
contributions: Data-driven stochastic UTOPF formulation for DOE computation.
claims: Improves day-ahead envelope quality under PV variability.
conclusions: Stochastic treatment outperforms static deterministic bounds in volatile PV settings.
limitations: Quality depends on scenario generation and forecast quality.
future_work: Couple day-ahead and intraday updates.

title: Dynamic Operating Envelopes in Unbalanced Distribution Systems: Comparison of Power Flow Models and Solution Methods
summary: Benchmarks DOE outcomes across alternative unbalanced power-flow models and solver strategies to quantify fidelity-speed tradeoffs.
key_equations: Model family comparison around AC, linearized AC, and approximate DistFlow constraints.
assumptions: Same feeder data and operating snapshots across methods.
Solver tolerances harmonized for fair comparison.
contributions: Systematic comparison of model/solver choices for DOE workflows.
claims: Model choice materially changes envelope size and violation risk.
conclusions: No single model dominates all operating conditions; context-aware selection is needed.
limitations: Results depend on tested feeder set and operating scenarios.
future_work: Auto-selection/hybridization of DOE solvers by condition.

title: Data-Driven Dynamic Operating Envelope in LV Distribution Networks with Smart Meter Data
summary: Uses smart-meter-driven models to estimate dynamic envelopes in LV networks, reducing dependence on fully instrumented state estimation.
key_equations: Learning-based mapping: u_t = f_\theta(z_t) with security post-check g(x_t,u_t)\le0
assumptions: Smart meter data quality is sufficient for model training.
Operational constraints are enforced downstream or in training.
contributions: Data-driven DOE workflow leveraging AMI data.
Quantifies practical deployment potential where telemetry is limited.
claims: Data-driven DOE can approximate optimization-based envelopes at lower operational cost.
conclusions: AMI-informed methods are promising when validated with physical constraints.
limitations: Model drift and dataset shift risks.
future_work: Online retraining and uncertainty-aware prediction intervals.

title: Interpretable Dynamic Operating Envelopes in Distribution Systems via Deep Learning
summary: Develops an interpretable deep-learning approach for DOEs with sensitivity-based explanations linked to network conditions.
key_equations: Interpretable surrogate: u = f_\theta(z),\ partial u/\partial z_i interpreted as feature influence
assumptions: Training data spans relevant operating conditions.
Interpretability metrics correlate with physical sensitivities.
contributions: Interpretable ML DOE model and evaluation protocol.
claims: Interpretability can improve operator trust without major accuracy loss.
conclusions: Explainable surrogates are viable complements to OPF-based computation.
limitations: Out-of-distribution behavior remains a concern.
future_work: Hybrid OPF-ML envelopes with certifiable safeguards.

title: Allocation Dynamic Operating Envelopes to DERs in Distribution Networks Considering Technical and Equitable Criteria
summary: Studies DOE allocation with technical feasibility and fairness objectives, highlighting equity impacts on DER curtailment distribution.
key_equations: \alpha-fair utility: U(u)=\sum_i w_i\frac{u_i^{1-\alpha}}{1-\alpha}
Envelope allocation OP: \max_u U(u)\ 	ext{s.t. network constraints}
assumptions: Fairness encoded via selected utility function.
Network model sufficiently accurate for allocation-level decisions.
contributions: Formal fairness-aware DOE allocation framework.
claims: Fairness criteria significantly re-shape envelopes compared with purely technical maxima.
conclusions: Distributional outcomes should be explicit design choices in DOE policy.
limitations: Stakeholder-specific fairness definitions can conflict.
future_work: Participatory fairness parameter selection and regulatory integration.
evidence: DOI; cited in MDPI review with fairness focus

title: Dynamic Operating Envelope-Enabled Peer-to-Peer Trading in Distribution Networks
summary: Couples DOE constraints with P2P market clearing to enforce network security during local trading.
key_equations: Market clearing with envelopes: \max_{p} W(p)\ 	ext{s.t. } p_i \in [\underline{u}_i,\overline{u}_i]
assumptions: Participants accept DOE-constrained bids.
Network constraints represented in clearing.
contributions: Integrates DOE policy into local market mechanism design.
claims: DOE-aware P2P trading reduces violation risk while retaining market flexibility.
conclusions: Local markets and envelopes are complementary when jointly optimized.
limitations: Behavioral and participation uncertainties not fully modeled.
future_work: Real-world pilot evaluation with dynamic participation.

title: Bargaining-Based Dynamic Operating Envelopes Allocation Method in Distribution Networks
summary: Uses bargaining/game-theoretic mechanisms to allocate envelopes among DER participants under network constraints.
key_equations: Nash bargaining objective: \max \prod_i (u_i-u_i^0)
Equivalent convex form: \max \sum_i \log(u_i-u_i^0)
assumptions: Disagreement points are well-defined.
Participants are rational and bargaining-compatible.
contributions: Bargaining mechanism for envelope allocation fairness-efficiency tradeoff.
claims: Bargaining can improve perceived fairness and acceptance.
conclusions: Mechanism design is central for deployable DOE allocation.
limitations: Requires robust parameterization of disagreement utilities.
future_work: Incentive compatibility under strategic behavior.
evidence: DOI record from MDPI review references

title: Dynamic-Operating-Envelope-Integrated Cooperative Energy Trading and Voltage Regulation in Distribution Networks
summary: Jointly optimizes trading and voltage regulation with DOE constraints in cooperative settings.
key_equations: Bi-level/cooperative objective with voltage constraints v_{min}\le v\le v_{max}.
assumptions: Coordination framework between market and network control exists.
contributions: Unified optimization of energy trading and secure operation via DOE.
claims: Cooperative optimization increases welfare while respecting grid limits.
conclusions: DOE can be embedded into advanced local coordination frameworks.
limitations: Coordination overhead may be high in practice.
future_work: Distributed implementations with communication constraints.

title: Dynamic Operating Envelope-Based Local Energy Market for Prosumers with EVs in Distribution Networks
summary: Investigates DOE-informed local energy markets with EV flexibility and network-security constraints.
key_equations: EV-constrained market optimization subject to envelope bounds and network constraints.
assumptions: EV availability and user preferences can be represented in market model.
contributions: Integrates EV flexibility into DOE-constrained market operation.
claims: EV flexibility under DOE constraints can improve utilization without violating limits.
conclusions: DOE can act as a practical interface between network operation and market dispatch.
limitations: Dependence on EV behavior forecasting.
future_work: Real-time EV uncertainty handling and user incentive design.

title: A Framework for Prosumer-Centric Peer-to-Peer Trading in Distribution Networks with Dynamic Network-Secure Limits
summary: Defines network-secure dynamic limits for prosumer-centric P2P trading in distribution systems.
key_equations: Secure limit coupling: p_i\in\mathcal{U}_i(t),\ sum_i p_i with AC network feasibility constraints.
assumptions: Distribution-level market can enforce network-secure limits.
contributions: Prosumer-centric trading framework anchored by dynamic secure bounds.
claims: Security limits preserve operation while enabling market flexibility.
conclusions: Policy-compatible market design requires explicit network-security interfaces.
limitations: Implementation requires DSO-market coordination protocols.
future_work: Field-trial integration with existing market platforms.
evidence: Applied Energy DOI from review source

title: Development and Validation of Dynamic Operating Envelope Enabled Demand Response in Distribution Networks
summary: Validates DOE-enabled demand response operation with practical workflow and network-security criteria.
key_equations: Demand response scheduling under envelope constraints and voltage/current limits.
assumptions: Responsive loads follow dispatch signals within modeled flexibility bounds.
contributions: Validation-oriented DOE demand response framework.
claims: DOE-enabled DR improves flexibility while controlling violations.
conclusions: DOE can operationalize secure DR in distribution systems.
limitations: Validation context may not cover all feeder archetypes.
future_work: Broader pilots and integration with tariff/incentive mechanisms.

title: Time-Varying Operating Regions of End-Users and Feeders in Low-Voltage Distribution Networks
summary: Defines time-varying operating regions for end-users/feeders, closely related to dynamic envelope concepts.
key_equations: Operating region: \mathcal{R}_t = \{p_t : \exists x_t, g(x_t,p_t)\le0\}.
assumptions: Operating region can be represented with tractable approximations.
contributions: Generalized feeder/user operating-region formulation.
claims: Time-varying regions better capture network flexibility than static limits.
conclusions: Temporal adaptation is essential for high-DER feeders.
limitations: Relies on model and forecast quality.
future_work: Uncertainty-aware and market-integrated operating regions.

title: Operating-Envelopes-Aware Decentralized Welfare Maximization
venue: Allerton Conference on Communication, Control, and Computing
summary: Studies decentralized welfare optimization under operating-envelope constraints.
key_equations: Decentralized welfare objective with envelope feasibility constraints.
assumptions: Agents optimize locally under communicated envelope signals.
contributions: Decentralized framework tying welfare maximization to envelope controls.
claims: Decentralization can preserve welfare while respecting network limits.
conclusions: Envelope-aware decomposition is promising for scalable coordination.
limitations: Communication and convergence constraints in practice.
future_work: Asynchronous and robust decentralized variants.

title: A Bargaining-Based Dynamic Operating Envelopes Allocation Method in Distribution Networks
summary: Conference precursor introducing bargaining-based DOE allocation.
key_equations: Bargaining optimization over envelope variables under network constraints.
assumptions: Negotiation protocol converges to feasible allocation.
contributions: Early conference validation of bargaining approach.
claims: Bargaining improves equity-efficiency balance.
conclusions: Method warrants extended journal treatment.

title: Exact Optimal Power Dispatch in Unbalanced Distribution Systems With High PV Penetration
summary: Develops exact OPF formulations for unbalanced systems with high PV penetration, foundational for accurate envelope computation.
key_equations: Unbalanced branch-flow constraints in phase domain.
SOCP relaxation exactness conditions.
assumptions: Radial structure and operating conditions meeting exactness requirements.
contributions: Exact/tractable OPF approach in unbalanced feeders.
claims: Convex relaxation can be exact in relevant regimes.
conclusions: Enables computationally tractable optimization with strong fidelity guarantees.
limitations: Exactness not universal for all loading/topologies.
future_work: Broaden exactness to wider network classes.

title: Network-Level Optimization for Unbalanced Power Distribution System: Approximation and Relaxation
summary: Analyzes approximation/relaxation methods for unbalanced network-level optimization relevant to scalable DOE computation.
key_equations: Approximate model: F(x)\approx \tilde{F}(x)
Relaxed feasible set: \mathcal{X}\subseteq\tilde{\mathcal{X}}
assumptions: Approximation error remains controlled over operating region.
contributions: Error-aware approximation and relaxation framework.
claims: Approximation can preserve decision quality under defined conditions.
conclusions: Useful tradeoff path between full AC fidelity and scalability.
limitations: Error bounds may widen under stressed operating conditions.
future_work: Adaptive approximation switching with condition monitoring.

title: Online Voltage Control for Unbalanced Distribution Networks Using Projected Newton Method
summary: Develops online projected-Newton voltage control for unbalanced networks, relevant for real-time envelope enforcement loops.
key_equations: Projected Newton step: x^{k+1}=\Pi_{\mathcal{X}}(x^k-\alpha_k H^{-1}\nabla f(x^k))
assumptions: Local Hessian approximation sufficiently accurate and invertible.
contributions: Fast online control algorithm for unbalanced distribution voltage regulation.
claims: Projected Newton improves convergence speed over first-order methods.
conclusions: Second-order online methods are promising for distribution control.
limitations: Computation/communication constraints in embedded deployment.
future_work: Distributed second-order variants with limited communication.

title: Hierarchical Optimal Power Flow Algorithm in Three-Phase Unbalanced Distribution Networks Based on Improved Gradient Method
venue: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
summary: Presents hierarchical OPF decomposition for unbalanced networks with improved gradient updates, relevant for scalable DOE solvers.
key_equations: Hierarchical decomposition: \min_x f(x)=\sum_a f_a(x_a) + coupling terms
assumptions: Hierarchy partitions network with manageable coupling.
contributions: Improved-gradient hierarchical OPF for unbalanced networks.
claims: Improves scalability while maintaining feasible operation.
conclusions: Hierarchical optimization is suitable for large feeder coordination.
limitations: Performance sensitive to partitioning quality.
future_work: Adaptive partitioning and asynchronous updates.

title: Taylor-Expansion-Based Robust Power Flow for Three-Phase Unbalanced Distribution Networks
summary: Uses Taylor expansion to derive robust approximations for unbalanced robust power flow.
key_equations: First-order expansion: g(x,\xi)\approx g(x,\bar{\xi})+\nabla_\xi g(x,\bar{\xi})(\xi-\bar{\xi})
assumptions: Operating point and perturbation magnitudes keep expansion valid.
contributions: Robust PF approximation method for unbalanced systems.
claims: Taylor-based robustification can reduce computational burden.
conclusions: Approximation offers practical tradeoffs for uncertainty handling.
limitations: Accuracy can degrade under large uncertainty excursions.
future_work: Higher-order or adaptive expansion methods.

title: A Distributed Approach in Solving Optimal Power Flow in Three-Phase Unbalanced Networks
summary: Introduces a distributed OPF strategy in three-phase unbalanced networks, relevant for decentralized DOE computations.
key_equations: Distributed update form with coupling multipliers (ADMM-style decomposition).
assumptions: Problem decomposition aligns with network partitions.
Reliable communication among agents.
contributions: Distributed OPF for three-phase unbalanced settings.
claims: Distributed methods can scale better than centralized OPF.
conclusions: Decentralized optimization is viable for unbalanced OPF.
limitations: Convergence speed depends on tuning and communication latency.
future_work: Robust/distributed implementations under packet loss and delay.
evidence: DOI metadata and citation chain from related works

title: A Distributed Approach in Solving OPF in Three-Phase Unbalanced Networks
summary: Thesis-level derivation of distributed OPF for unbalanced systems with implementation and algorithmic detail.
key_equations: Lagrangian decomposition of OPF into local subproblems with dual coordination.
assumptions: Convexified/local approximations support decomposition.
contributions: Detailed derivation and implementation-oriented distributed OPF treatment.
claims: Distributed OPF is practical for unbalanced systems under proper decomposition.
conclusions: Algorithmic decomposition can reduce central solver burden.
limitations: Dependent on model assumptions and test systems used.

title: Optimal Reactive Power Dispatch for Voltage Regulation in Unbalanced Distribution Systems
summary: Addresses unbalanced reactive power optimization for voltage control, foundational for envelope feasibility enforcement.
key_equations: Voltage objective with reactive dispatch: \min_q ||v-v^{ref}||_2^2 + \lambda ||q||_2^2
assumptions: Reactive capability limits modeled for DER inverters.
Unbalanced network model retained.
contributions: Optimization framework for unbalanced voltage regulation.
claims: Coordinated reactive dispatch can effectively regulate unbalanced voltage profiles.
conclusions: Optimization-based VAR control supports high-DER operation.
limitations: Requires communication/control infrastructure and model calibration.
future_work: Distributed and uncertainty-aware VAR optimization.

title: Branch Flow Model: Relaxations and Convexification—Part I
summary: Introduces branch-flow OPF relaxations, a core foundation for tractable distribution optimization.
key_equations: P_{ij}=\sum_{k:j\to k}P_{jk}+p_j+r_{ij}l_{ij}
Q_{ij}=\sum_{k:j\to k}Q_{jk}+q_j+x_{ij}l_{ij}
v_j=v_i-2(r_{ij}P_{ij}+x_{ij}Q_{ij})+(r_{ij}^2+x_{ij}^2)l_{ij}
l_{ij}v_i=P_{ij}^2+Q_{ij}^2
assumptions: Radial network emphasis.
Relaxation exactness under suitable conditions.
contributions: Branch-flow formalism and convexification pathway.
claims: Convex relaxations can solve OPF exactly under practical conditions.
conclusions: Branch-flow model is central to distribution OPF tractability.
limitations: Exactness depends on topology and operating region.
future_work: Generalization to broader mesh/unbalanced settings.
evidence: DOI metadata validated by web search

title: Branch Flow Model: Relaxations and Convexification—Part II
summary: Extends branch-flow relaxation analysis and convexification details, including implementation aspects.
key_equations: Convexified OPF under branch-flow variables and SOCP structure.
assumptions: Similar to Part I with additional convexification constraints.
contributions: Complementary convexification and algorithmic treatment.
claims: Demonstrates practical solvability with convexified branch-flow model.
conclusions: Supports broad use of branch-flow relaxations in distribution OPF.
limitations: Exactness conditions remain scenario-dependent.
future_work: Unbalanced and uncertain extensions.
evidence: DOI metadata validated by web search

title: Exact Convex Relaxation of Optimal Power Flow in Radial Networks
summary: Provides exactness conditions for convex OPF in radial networks, foundational to envelope-optimization tractability arguments.
key_equations: SOCP-relaxed OPF and exactness conditions on load bounds and voltage profiles.
assumptions: Radial topology and condition-specific loading assumptions.
contributions: Formal exactness guarantees for convex radial OPF.
claims: Under stated conditions, convex relaxation yields global optimum.
conclusions: Gives theory underpinning many practical radial OPF tools.
limitations: Conditions may fail in stressed or unusual regimes.
future_work: Relaxed conditions and broader network applicability.

title: Equivalent Relaxations of Optimal Power Flow
summary: Analyzes equivalence relationships among OPF relaxations used in power system optimization.
key_equations: Mappings between SDP/SOCP and branch-flow-based relaxations.
assumptions: Standard OPF problem structure and regularity assumptions.
contributions: Unifies relaxation perspectives and equivalence proofs.
claims: Different convex relaxations can be equivalent under specific conditions.
conclusions: Guides method choice for computational efficiency and guarantees.
limitations: Equivalence conditions are not universal.

title: Zero Duality Gap in Optimal Power Flow Problem
summary: Landmark result on conditions for zero duality gap in OPF, motivating convex optimization approaches.
key_equations: SDP-relaxed OPF primal-dual pair with rank conditions.
assumptions: Network and cost structure satisfy sufficient conditions for exactness.
contributions: Theoretical basis for convex OPF exactness in many practical cases.
claims: Duality gap can vanish in realistic OPF instances.
conclusions: Convex relaxations are foundational for scalable OPF.
limitations: Exactness can fail outside assumptions.
future_work: Sharper exactness characterizations in broader grids.

title: Exactness of OPF Relaxation in Three-Phase Radial Networks Under Delta Connections
summary: Extends OPF exactness analysis to practical three-phase radial networks with delta connections.
key_equations: Three-phase branch-flow relaxation with delta-connection constraints.
assumptions: Radial topology and admissible operating region conditions.
contributions: Exactness conditions in richer three-phase connection models.
claims: Convex relaxation remains exact in important practical cases with delta loads.
conclusions: Supports convex approaches in realistic unbalanced feeder models.
limitations: Condition sensitivity remains a practical concern.
future_work: Robust exactness under uncertainty and mixed topologies.
